Cardiff Coffee
The Story Ward Explorer All Cafés About

A City
in a Cup.
Coffee, Cost
& Culture.

Cardiff has 28 electoral wards. We found an independent café in each one — mapping the city's coffee, one neighbourhood at a time.

By Samuel & Aanchal · May 2026
28Wards
28Cafés
Scroll to read

Cardiff is a Welsh capital made up of 28 wonderful wards. Each one is home to an independent coffee shop that has their own identity, community, and most importantly coffee.

This project started with a great question: where is the cheapest coffee in Cardiff? We didn't want to look at major chains that have 3 stores on the same street. We wanted to look at shops with their own name, character, and regulars who'd miss it if it closed.

What we found surprised us. From the Grindfather in Pontprennau to Buds Coffee in Radyr; from the theatrical chairs of Sparrow in Adamsdown to the bakery warmth of Green Door in Pentwyn, Cardiff's coffee map stretches far further than most people realise. Shops like Sparrow in Adamsdown thrive off the bonds built with customers, they said "nothing makes us happier than seeing relationships form between people who meet through the shop".

Sibling, an independent coffee shop in the heart of Plasnewydd, is run by actual siblings. We met Rowan and Gwenllian from their team. They told us how they offer three different espressos at once, rotating between Freda, Red Bank from Kendal, and Triple Co from Bristol, with their own Cardiff-blended Cafeina as the base, consisting of a taste of milk chocolate, cranberries and hazelnut. Alongside the coffee they offer house-made sourdough, house-made cakes, a wine list, and regular events. Sibling is a perfect example of Cardiff's community thriving and how siblings can create something special in between the arguments.

These are the kinds of details that don't show up on Google Maps. This project is an attempt to surface them, ward by ward.

Does where you live change the price of your coffee?

The average house price in Caerau, in the west, sits around £109,000. In Lisvane and Thornhill, in the north, it's over £554,000. That's a big difference.

We wanted to know: does the postcode you drink your coffee in affect what you pay for it? Our early data suggests yes, though the relationship is not as easy as a simple line graph that rises as the houses get more expensive. Cafés in wealthier wards tend to charge more, but not always. Ford Cafe in Llanrumney (average house price: £243k) charges £2.50 for an americano, the cheapest we found. Meanwhile some city-centre spots in mid-range wards push well past £3.50.

Each card in the explorer below shows the average house price for that ward alongside the café's drink prices. The gap shows what kind of neighbourhood this is, and what kind of café has decided to open there. We'll keep updating the data as more cafés respond.

The Data

Does your postcode change the price of your coffee?

Each circle is a café. The further right, the wealthier the ward. The higher up, the pricier the latte. Hover for details. 13 of 28 cafés have confirmed prices so far — data ongoing.

Samuel & Aanchal  ·  Cardiff Coffee Project  ·  2026  ·  Built with ☕ and Leaflet.js

About this project

How we built Cardiff Coffee — and what we used to do it

Cardiff Coffee is a data journalism project created by Samuel and Aanchal as part of their MSc in Computational and Data Journalism at Cardiff University. The aim: visit one independent café in each of Cardiff's 28 electoral wards, collect price data, and ask whether postcode shapes what you pay for a cup of coffee.

To complete this project we gathered data from the coffee shops themselves. We ensured each shop we looked at had an active Instagram page and messaged each asking for the details. We visited each café in person between March and May 2026, recording prices from the menu, talking to owners, and photographing the spaces. For the average house prices we used reliable letting agent websites such as Zoopla and Rightmove, cross-referenced with HM Land Registry ward-level averages (2023–24).

All images used in the project are taken from the Instagram and Facebook pages of the coffee shops, with the exception of Sparrow and Sibling, which we photographed ourselves.

How it was built

The site is a single-file interactive feature built with HTML, CSS and vanilla JavaScript. The ward map uses Leaflet.js with Cardiff ward GeoJSON data. The scrollytelling explorer pairs Leaflet with a scroll-detection system that activates each café card as you read down the page. The scatter plot is drawn with raw SVG — no charting library. All café photos are embedded directly in the HTML as base64, so the page works without a server.

AI disclosure

To aid us in the completion of this project we used Artificial Intelligence tools such as Claude, to help us work efficiently, as well as help with the design and polishing of the website. Claude was used primarily to build and debug the interactive components: the Leaflet map, the scrollytelling logic, the scatter plot, and the community pin-drop feature. All editorial decisions — which cafés to include, what questions to ask, how to frame the data — were made by us. The café copy and article text were written by us and reviewed by our supervisors. AI was not used to generate any of the written journalism in this piece.

Data sources

Coffee prices
Collected directly from café owners via Instagram, March–May 2026.
House prices
Zoopla & Rightmove, cross-referenced with HM Land Registry ward-level averages (2023–24).
Ward boundaries
Cardiff Council ward GeoJSON — 28 electoral wards.
Photography
Instagram & Facebook pages of each café. Sparrow and Sibling photographed by Samuel & Aanchal.

Samuel & Aanchal  ·  MSc Computational and Data Journalism, Cardiff University  ·  May 2026
